revision.comment: 静間神社の紹介（新規）
revision.text:
静間神社は８８６年（仁和２年）創建の延喜式に載せられた式内社である。戦前は４つの村に氏子をかかえていたため郷社であった。 所在地は島根県大田市静間町（垂水）1765番地であり、本殿は春日造りである。 主祭神はオオナムチノミコト、スクナヒコナノミコトであり、明治期以降、村内にあった厳島神社（２社）、八幡宮（２社）、大年神社、枯れ木神社等が本殿に合祀されている。 境内社としては東照宮、天満宮、水神社がある。 例祭は４月15日（明治期までは旧暦の３月と９月の15 日）である。
本社は創建時には近くの魚津集落にある万葉集ゆかりの静之窟内に祀られていたが、高波による被害を受け、一度窟外に出た後、1674年（延宝２年）６月の豪雨により山津波が発生し社殿が崩壊したため、翌年垂水山に遷座された。
1650年以降の棟札が１０４枚も保存されており、この棟札を見ると苗字・帯刀の許されなかった江戸時代においても
、神社活動においては住民は苗字を使いいきいきと生活していたことが窺がえる。

revision.comment: +{{出典の明記}} +{{神社}} +{{Shinto-stub}}
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2022年11月}} {{神社 |名称 = 静間神社 |画像 = |所在地 = |ISO = JP-32 | 緯度度 = 35 |緯度分 = 12 |緯度秒 = 6.6 | 経度度 = 132 |経度分 = 27 |経度秒 = 20.6 |ラベル位置 = |地図 = Japan Shimane |祭神 = {{Ublist| オオナムチノミコト | スクナヒコナノミコト }} |神体 = |社格 = [[郷社]] |創建 = [[仁和]]2年（[[886年]]） |宮司 = |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = 4月15日 |神事 = }} '''静間神社'''（しずまじんじゃ）は[[886年]]（[[仁和]]2年）創建の[[延喜式]]に載せられた[[式内社]]である。戦前は4つの村に氏子をかかえていたため[[郷社]]であった。所在地は[[島根県]][[大田市]]静間町（垂水）1765番地であり、本殿は春日造りである。主祭神はオオナムチノミコト、スクナヒコナノミコトであり、明治期以降、村内にあった厳島神社（2社）、八幡宮（2社）、大年神社、枯れ木神社等が本殿に合祀されている。境内社としては東照宮、天満宮、水神社がある。例祭は4月15日（明治期までは旧暦の3月と9月の15日）である。
本社は創建時には近くの魚津集落にある万葉集ゆかりの静之窟内に祀られていたが、高波による被害を受け、一度窟外に出た後、[[1674年]]（[[延宝]]2年）6月の豪雨により山津波が発生し社殿が崩壊したため、翌年垂水山に遷座された。
1650年以降の棟札が104枚も保存されており、この棟札を見ると苗字・帯刀の許されなかった江戸時代においても、神社活動においては住民は苗字を使いいきいきと生活していたことが窺がえる。

revision.comment: Wikipediaに静間神社の項目が無かったので１１月５日、１１月23日と今回の３回で参考文献も含めて記述した。
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2022年11月}} '''静間神社'''（しずまじんじゃ）は島根県大田市静間町（垂水）1765番地に所在する万葉集に歌われた「志都乃石室」ゆかりの古社であり、延喜式に載せられた[[式内社]]である<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>。
== 解説 == 主祭神はオオナムチノミコト、スクナヒコナノミコトであり、明治期以降、村内にあった八幡宮（2社）、天満宮（2社）、大年神社、枯れ木神社等が本殿に合祀されている。
境内社としては、左社に天満宮（元は垂水八幡宮であったが上静間八幡宮と合祀し本殿へ遷座したため、現在は滝の前にあった天神様を祀る）、右社に東照宮（旧天領であったため徳川家康と毛利輝元を祀る）、池の中に水神社（元は宇賀御魂神社であり倉稲魂が祭神であったがこの神は本殿に祀られている。厳島神社もここにあったと推測される。）がある。 例祭は4月15日（明治期までは旧暦の3月と9月の15日）である。
本社は創建時には近くの魚津集落にある万葉集ゆかりの静之窟内に祀られていたが、高波による被害を受け、一度窟外に出た後、[[1674年]]（[[延宝]]2年）6月の豪雨により山津波が発生し社殿が崩壊したため、翌年に垂水山に遷座された。
社内には1650年以降の棟札が104枚も保存されており、この棟札を見ると苗字・帯刀の許されなかった江戸時代においても、神社活動においては住民は苗字を使いいきいきと生活していたことが窺がえる。
参考文献
『静之窟』 石川勝貞著 1917年
『式内社調査報告２１巻』 式内社調査研究会著 1976年
『静間神社の由来と鳥居』 澤 勲・石川勝典・肥塚義明著 洞窟環境NET学会 2013年
脚注<references /> == 関連項目 == * [[石見国の式内社一覧]]

{{出典の明記|date=2022年11月}} '''静間神社'''（しずまじんじゃ）は島根県大田市静間町（垂水）1765番地に所在する万葉集に歌われた「志都乃石室」ゆかりの古社であり、延喜式に載せられた[[式内社]]である<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>。
== 解説 == 仁和２年（８８６年）創建であり、現在の本殿は春日づくりである。
主祭神はオオナムチノミコト、スクナヒコナノミコトであり、明治期以降、村内にあった八幡宮（2社）、天満宮（2社）、大年神社、枯れ木神社等が本殿に合祀されている。
境内社としては、左社に天満宮（元は垂水八幡宮であったが上静間八幡宮と合祀し本殿へ遷座したため、現在は滝の前にあった天神様を祀る）、右社に東照宮（旧天領であったため徳川家康と毛利輝元を祀る）、池の中に水神社（元は宇賀御魂神社であり倉稲魂が祭神であったがこの神は本殿に祀られている。厳島神社もここにあったと推測される。）がある。 例祭は4月15日（明治期までは旧暦の3月と9月の15日）である。
本社は創建時には近くの魚津集落にある万葉集ゆかりの静之窟内に祀られていたが、高波による被害を受け、一度窟外に出た後、[[1674年]]（[[延宝]]2年）6月の豪雨により山津波が発生し社殿が崩壊したため、翌年に垂水山に遷座された。
社内には1650年以降の棟札が104枚も保存されており、この棟札を見ると苗字・帯刀の許されなかった江戸時代においても、神社活動においては住民は苗字を使いいきいきと生活していたことが窺がえる。
参考文献
『静之窟』 石川勝貞著 1917年
『式内社調査報告２１巻』 式内社調査研究会著 1976年
『静間神社の由来と鳥居』 澤 勲・石川勝典・肥塚義明著 洞窟環境NET学会 2013年

revision.comment: 3文字削除
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2022年11月}} '''静間神社'''（しずまじんじゃ）は島根県大田市静間町（垂水）1765番地に所在する万葉集に歌われた「志都乃石室」ゆかりの古社であり、延喜式に載せられた[[式内社]]である<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>。
== 解説 == 仁和2年（886年）創建であり、現在の本殿は春日づくりである。
主祭神はオオナムチノミコト、スクナヒコナノミコトであり、明治期以降、村内にあった八幡宮（2社）、厳島神社（2社）、大年神社、枯れ木神社等が本殿に合祀されている。
境内社としては、左社に天満宮（元は垂水八幡宮であったが上静間八幡宮と合祀し本殿へ遷座したため、現在は滝の前にあった天神様を祀る）、右社に東照宮（旧天領であったため徳川家康と毛利輝元を祀る）、池の中に水神社（元は宇賀御魂神社であり倉稲魂が祭神であったがこの神は本殿に祀られている。厳島神社もここにあったと推測される。）がある。 例祭は4月15日（明治期までは旧暦の3月と9月の15日）である。
本社は創建時には近くの魚津集落にある万葉集ゆかりの静之窟内に祀られていたが、高波による被害を受け、一度窟外に出た後、[[1674年]]（[[延宝]]2年）6月の豪雨により山津波が発生し社殿が崩壊したため、翌年に垂水山に遷座された。
社内には1650年以降の棟札が104枚も保存されており、この棟札を見ると苗字・帯刀の許されなかった江戸時代においても、神社活動においては住民は苗字を使いいきいきと生活していたことが窺がえる。
参考文献
『静之窟』 石川勝貞著 1917年
『式内社調査報告２１巻』 式内社調査研究会著 1976年
『静間神社の由来と鳥居』 澤 勲・石川勝典・肥塚義明著 洞窟環境NET学会 2013年
脚注<references /> == 関連項目 == * [[石見国の式内社一覧]]

{{nihongo|'''Shizuma Shrine'''|静間神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

== instance of (P31) ==

* {{ill|Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|WD=Q134917286}}
* {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|en|Shikinai Shosha|ja|式内小社|WD=Q134917287}}

* {{ill|Kokuhei Shosha (Engishiki)|WD=Q135206465}}

{{nihongo|'''Shizuma Shrine'''|静間神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Shizuma Shrine'''|静間神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Shizuma Shrine'''|静間神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Shizuma Shrine'''|静間神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

revision.comment: 14 revisions imported
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2022年11月}} {{神社 |名称=静間神社 |画像={{画像募集中|cat=大田市}} |所在地=[[島根県]][[大田市]]静間町垂水1765番地 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神=[[大己貴命]]・[[少彦名命]] |例祭=4月15日 |創建=[[886年]]（[[仁和]]2年） |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=静間神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''静間神社'''（しずまじんじゃ）は、[[島根県]][[大田市]]静間町に所在する[[万葉集]]に歌われた「志都乃石室」ゆかりの古社であり、[[延喜式]]に載せられた[[式内社]]である<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>。
== 解説 == [[仁和]]2年（886年）創建であり、現在の本殿は春日づくりである。
主祭神は[[大国主|オオナムチノミコト]]、[[スクナビコナ|スクナヒコナノミコト]]であり、[[明治]]期以降、村内にあった八幡宮（2社）、厳島神社（2社）、大年神社、枯れ木神社等が本殿に合祀されている。
境内社としては、左社に天満宮（元は垂水八幡宮であったが上静間八幡宮と合祀し本殿へ遷座したため、現在は滝の前にあった天神様を祀る）、右社に東照宮（旧[[天領]]であったため[[徳川家康]]と[[毛利輝元]]を祀る）、池の中に水神社（元は宇賀御魂神社であり[[ウカノミタマ|倉稲魂]]が祭神であったがこの神は本殿に祀られている。厳島神社もここにあったと推測される。）がある。[[例祭]]は4月15日（[[明治]]期までは旧暦の3月と9月の15日）である。
本社は創建時には近くの魚津集落にある万葉集ゆかりの[[静之窟]]内に祀られていたが、高波による被害を受け、一度窟外に出た後、[[1674年]]（[[延宝]]2年）6月の豪雨により[[土石流|山津波]]が発生し社殿が崩壊したため、翌年に垂水山に遷座された。
社内には1650年以降の棟札が104枚も保存されており、この棟札を見ると[[苗字帯刀|苗字・帯刀]]の許されなかった[[江戸時代]]においても、神社活動においては住民は[[名字|苗字]]を使いいきいきと生活していたことが窺がえる。
== 参考文献 ==
* 『静之窟』 石川勝貞著 1917年 NCID BA32916118
* 『式内社調査報告２１巻』 式内社調査研究会著 1976年 NCID BN00231541
* 『静間神社の由来と鳥居』 澤勲・石川勝典・肥塚義明著 洞窟環境NET学会 2013年 ISSN 1884-2402
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}}<references /> == 関連項目 == * [[石見国の式内社一覧]]
{{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しすましんしや}} [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:大田市の建築物]] [[Category:大田市の歴史]] [[Category:石見国の式内社]]

== Japanese Wikipedia content == {{出典の明記|date=2022年11月}} {{神社 |名称=静間神社 |画像={{画像募集中|cat=大田市}} |所在地=[[島根県]][[大田市]]静間町垂水1765番地 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神=[[大己貴命]]・[[少彦名命]] |例祭=4月15日 |創建=[[886年]]（[[仁和]]2年） |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=静間神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''静間神社'''（しずまじんじゃ）は、[[島根県]][[大田市]]静間町に所在する[[万葉集]]に歌われた「志都乃石室」ゆかりの古社であり、[[延喜式]]に載せられた[[式内社]]である<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>。
== 解説 == [[仁和]]2年（886年）創建であり、現在の本殿は春日づくりである。
主祭神は[[大国主|オオナムチノミコト]]、[[スクナビコナ|スクナヒコナノミコト]]であり、[[明治]]期以降、村内にあった八幡宮（2社）、厳島神社（2社）、大年神社、枯れ木神社等が本殿に合祀されている。
境内社としては、左社に天満宮（元は垂水八幡宮であったが上静間八幡宮と合祀し本殿へ遷座したため、現在は滝の前にあった天神様を祀る）、右社に東照宮（旧[[天領]]であったため[[徳川家康]]と[[毛利輝元]]を祀る）、池の中に水神社（元は宇賀御魂神社であり[[ウカノミタマ|倉稲魂]]が祭神であったがこの神は本殿に祀られている。厳島神社もここにあったと推測される。）がある。[[例祭]]は4月15日（[[明治]]期までは旧暦の3月と9月の15日）である。
本社は創建時には近くの魚津集落にある万葉集ゆかりの[[静之窟]]内に祀られていたが、高波による被害を受け、一度窟外に出た後、[[1674年]]（[[延宝]]2年）6月の豪雨により[[土石流|山津波]]が発生し社殿が崩壊したため、翌年に垂水山に遷座された。
社内には1650年以降の棟札が104枚も保存されており、この棟札を見ると[[苗字帯刀|苗字・帯刀]]の許されなかった[[江戸時代]]においても、神社活動においては住民は[[名字|苗字]]を使いいきいきと生活していたことが窺がえる。
== 参考文献 ==
* 『静之窟』 石川勝貞著 1917年 NCID BA32916118
* 『式内社調査報告２１巻』 式内社調査研究会著 1976年 NCID BN00231541
* 『静間神社の由来と鳥居』 澤勲・石川勝典・肥塚義明著 洞窟環境NET学会 2013年 ISSN 1884-2402
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}}<references /> == 関連項目 == * [[石見国の式内社一覧]]
{{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しすましんしや}} [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:大田市の建築物]] [[Category:大田市の歴史]] [[Category:石見国の式内社]]
==End Japanese==

== 解説 == [[仁和]]2年（886年）創建であり、現在の本殿は春日づくりである。
主祭神は[[大国主|オオナムチノミコト]]、[[スクナビコナ|スクナヒコナノミコト]]であり、[[明治]]期以降、村内にあった八幡宮（2社）、厳島神社（2社）、大年神社、枯れ木神社等が本殿に合祀されている。
境内社としては、左社に天満宮（元は垂水八幡宮であったが上静間八幡宮と合祀し本殿へ遷座したため、現在は滝の前にあった天神様を祀る）、右社に東照宮（旧[[天領]]であったため[[徳川家康]]と[[毛利輝元]]を祀る）、池の中に水神社（元は宇賀御魂神社であり[[ウカノミタマ|倉稲魂]]が祭神であったがこの神は本殿に祀られている。厳島神社もここにあったと推測される。）がある。[[例祭]]は4月15日（[[明治]]期までは旧暦の3月と9月の15日）である。
本社は創建時には近くの魚津集落にある万葉集ゆかりの[[静之窟]]内に祀られていたが、高波による被害を受け、一度窟外に出た後、[[1674年]]（[[延宝]]2年）6月の豪雨により[[土石流|山津波]]が発生し社殿が崩壊したため、翌年に垂水山に遷座された。
社内には1650年以降の棟札が104枚も保存されており、この棟札を見ると[[苗字帯刀|苗字・帯刀]]の許されなかった[[江戸時代]]においても、神社活動においては住民は[[名字|苗字]]を使いいきいきと生活していたことが窺がえる。
== 参考文献 ==
* 『静之窟』 石川勝貞著 1917年 NCID BA32916118
* 『式内社調査報告２１巻』 式内社調査研究会著 1976年 NCID BN00231541
* 『静間神社の由来と鳥居』 澤勲・石川勝典・肥塚義明著 洞窟環境NET学会 2013年 ISSN 1884-2402
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}}<references /> == 関連項目 == * [[石見国の式内社一覧]]
{{Shinto-stub}}
==End Japanese== [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:大田市の建築物]] [[Category:大田市の歴史]] [[Category:石見国の式内社]]

[[JA:静間神社]]

{{wikidata link|Q134930739}}

== Japanese content ==
{{出典の明記|date=2022年11月}}
{{神社
|名称=静間神社
|画像={{画像募集中|cat=大田市}}
|所在地={{jalink|島根県|Q132751}}{{jalink|大田市|Q384796}}静間町垂水1765番地
|位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|祭神={{jalink|大己貴命}}・{{jalink|少彦名命}}
|例祭=4月15日
|創建={{jalink|886年|Q24683}}（{{jalink|仁和|Q1346317}}2年）
|地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=静間神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''静間神社'''（しずまじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|島根県|Q132751}}{{jalink|大田市|Q384796}}静間町に所在する{{jalink|万葉集|Q834321}}に歌われた「志都乃石室」ゆかりの古社であり、{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}に載せられた{{jalink|式内社}}である<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>。

=== 解説 ===
{{jalink|仁和|Q1346317}}2年（886年）創建であり、現在の本殿は春日づくりである。　

主祭神は{{jalink|大国主|Q276944|オオナムチノミコト}}、{{jalink|スクナビコナ|Q3275687|スクナヒコナノミコト}}であり、{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}期以降、村内にあった八幡宮（2社）、厳島神社（2社）、大年神社、枯れ木神社等が本殿に合祀されている。

境内社としては、左社に天満宮（元は垂水八幡宮であったが上静間八幡宮と合祀し本殿へ遷座したため、現在は滝の前にあった天神様を祀る）、右社に東照宮（旧{{jalink|天領|Q1685411}}であったため{{jalink|徳川家康|Q171977}}と{{jalink|毛利輝元|Q1142657}}を祀る）、池の中に水神社（元は宇賀御魂神社であり{{jalink|ウカノミタマ|Q3080728|倉稲魂}}が祭神であったがこの神は本殿に祀られている。厳島神社もここにあったと推測される。）がある。{{jalink|例祭|Q11385469}}は4月15日（{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}期までは旧暦の3月と9月の15日）である。

本社は創建時には近くの魚津集落にある万葉集ゆかりの{{jalink|静之窟|Q11662872}}内に祀られていたが、高波による被害を受け、一度窟外に出た後、{{jalink|1674年|Q7620}}（{{jalink|延宝|Q1145735}}2年）6月の豪雨により{{jalink|土石流|Q1817963|山津波}}が発生し社殿が崩壊したため、翌年に垂水山に遷座された。

社内には1650年以降の棟札が104枚も保存されており、この棟札を見ると{{jalink|苗字帯刀|Q11616116|苗字・帯刀}}の許されなかった{{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}においても、神社活動においては住民は{{jalink|名字|Q69634220|苗字}}を使いいきいきと生活していたことが窺がえる。

=== 参考文献　 ===

* 『静之窟』　石川勝貞著　1917年　　NCID  BA32916118　　　　　

* 『式内社調査報告２１巻』　式内社調査研究会著　1976年　　NCID  BN00231541

* 『静間神社の由来と鳥居』　澤勲・石川勝典・肥塚義明著　洞窟環境NET学会　2013年   ISSN  1884-2402

=== 脚注 ===
{{脚注ヘルプ}}<references />
=== 関連項目 ===

境内社としては、左社に天満宮（元は垂水八幡宮であったが上静間八幡宮と合祀し本殿へ遷座したため、現在は滝の前にあった天神様を祀る）、右社に東照宮（旧{{jalink|天領|Q1685411}}であったため{{jalink|徳川家康|Q171977}}と{{jalink|毛利輝元|Q1142657}}を祀る）、池の中に水神社（元は宇賀御魂神社であり{{jalink|ウカノミタマ|Q3080728|倉稲魂}}が祭神であったがこの神は本殿に祀られている。厳島神社もここにあったと推測される。）がある。{{jalink|例祭|Q11385469}}は4月15日（{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}期までは旧暦の3月と9月の15日）である。

本社は創建時には近くの魚津集落にある万葉集ゆかりの{{jalink|静之窟|Q11662872}}内に祀られていたが、高波による被害を受け、一度窟外に出た後、{{jalink|1674年|Q7620}}（{{jalink|延宝|Q1145735}}2年）6月の豪雨により{{jalink|土石流|Q1817963|山津波}}が発生し社殿が崩壊したため、翌年に垂水山に遷座された。

社内には1650年以降の棟札が104枚も保存されており、この棟札を見ると{{jalink|苗字帯刀|Q11616116|苗字・帯刀}}の許されなかった{{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}においても、神社活動においては住民は{{jalink|名字|Q69634220|苗字}}を使いいきいきと生活していたことが窺がえる。

=== 参考文献　 ===

* 『静之窟』　石川勝貞著　1917年　　NCID  BA32916118　　　　　

* 『式内社調査報告２１巻』　式内社調査研究会著　1976年　　NCID  BN00231541

* 『静間神社の由来と鳥居』　澤勲・石川勝典・肥塚義明著　洞窟環境NET学会　2013年   ISSN  1884-2402

[[JA:静間神社]]

{{wikidata link|Q134930739}}

== Japanese content ==
{{出典の明記|date=2022年11月}}
{{神社
|名称=静間神社
|画像={{画像募集中|cat=大田市}}
|所在地={{jalink|島根県|Q132751}}{{jalink|大田市|Q384796}}静間町垂水1765番地
|位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|祭神={{jalink|大己貴命}}・{{jalink|少彦名命}}
|例祭=4月15日
|創建={{jalink|886年|Q24683}}（{{jalink|仁和|Q1346317}}2年）
|地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=静間神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''静間神社'''（しずまじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|島根県|Q132751}}{{jalink|大田市|Q384796}}静間町に所在する{{jalink|万葉集|Q834321}}に歌われた「志都乃石室」ゆかりの古社であり、{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}に載せられた{{jalink|式内社}}である<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>。

=== 解説 ===
{{jalink|仁和|Q1346317}}2年（886年）創建であり、現在の本殿は春日づくりである。　

主祭神は{{jalink|大国主|Q276944|オオナムチノミコト}}、{{jalink|スクナビコナ|Q3275687|スクナヒコナノミコト}}であり、{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}期以降、村内にあった八幡宮（2社）、厳島神社（2社）、大年神社、枯れ木神社等が本殿に合祀されている。

境内社としては、左社に天満宮（元は垂水八幡宮であったが上静間八幡宮と合祀し本殿へ遷座したため、現在は滝の前にあった天神様を祀る）、右社に東照宮（旧{{jalink|天領|Q1685411}}であったため{{jalink|徳川家康|Q171977}}と{{jalink|毛利輝元|Q1142657}}を祀る）、池の中に水神社（元は宇賀御魂神社であり{{jalink|ウカノミタマ|Q3080728|倉稲魂}}が祭神であったがこの神は本殿に祀られている。厳島神社もここにあったと推測される。）がある。{{jalink|例祭|Q11385469}}は4月15日（{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}期までは旧暦の3月と9月の15日）である。

本社は創建時には近くの魚津集落にある万葉集ゆかりの{{jalink|静之窟|Q11662872}}内に祀られていたが、高波による被害を受け、一度窟外に出た後、{{jalink|1674年|Q7620}}（{{jalink|延宝|Q1145735}}2年）6月の豪雨により{{jalink|土石流|Q1817963|山津波}}が発生し社殿が崩壊したため、翌年に垂水山に遷座された。

社内には1650年以降の棟札が104枚も保存されており、この棟札を見ると{{jalink|苗字帯刀|Q11616116|苗字・帯刀}}の許されなかった{{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}においても、神社活動においては住民は{{jalink|名字|Q69634220|苗字}}を使いいきいきと生活していたことが窺がえる。

=== 参考文献　 ===

* 『静之窟』　石川勝貞著　1917年　　NCID  BA32916118　　　　　

* 『式内社調査報告２１巻』　式内社調査研究会著　1976年　　NCID  BN00231541

* 『静間神社の由来と鳥居』　澤勲・石川勝典・肥塚義明著　洞窟環境NET学会　2013年   ISSN  1884-2402

=== 脚注 ===
{{脚注ヘルプ}}<references />
=== 関連項目 ===
* {{jalink|石見国の式内社一覧|Q11587179}}

{{Shinto-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:しすましんしや}}

{{神社
|名称=Shizuma Shrine
|画像={{画像募集中|cat=Ōda}}
|所在地={{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Ōda|Q384796}} Shizuma-chō Tarumi 1765

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Shizuma Shrine''' (しずまじんじゃ) is an ancient shrine in Shizuma-chō, {{jalink|Ōda|Q384796}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}, associated with the “Shizu no Iwaya” sung in the {{jalink|Man'yōshū|Q834321}}, and is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>.

=== Explanation ===
It was founded in {{jalink|Ninna|Q1346317}} 2 (886), and the current main sanctuary is built in Kasuga-zukuri style.

The main deities are {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}} and {{jalink|Sukunabikona|Q3275687|Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}}. Since the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} period, two Hachimangū shrines, two Itsukushima shrines, Ōtoshi Shrine, Kareki Shrine, and others formerly in the village have been merged and enshrined in the main sanctuary.

As precinct shrines, there is Tenmangū on the left (formerly Tarumi Hachimangū, but after merger with Kami-Shizuma Hachimangū and transfer to the main sanctuary, it now enshrines the Tenjin deity that had stood before the waterfall); on the right is Tōshōgū (because it was former {{jalink|tenryō|Q1685411}}, it enshrines {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}} and {{jalink|Mōri Terumoto|Q1142657}}); and in the pond is Suijin Shrine (formerly Ukanomitama Shrine, with {{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Ukanomitama}} as deity, now enshrined in the main sanctuary; an Itsukushima Shrine is also presumed to have been here). The {{jalink|annual festival|Q11385469}} is April 15 (until the Meiji period, it was on the 15th days of the 3rd and 9th lunar months).

At the time of foundation, the shrine was enshrined inside {{jalink|Shizu no Kutsu|Q11662872}}, associated with the Man'yōshū, in the nearby Uozu settlement. After damage from high waves, it once moved outside the cave, then a heavy rain in June {{jalink|1674|Q7620}} ({{jalink|Enpō|Q1145735}} 2) caused a debris-flow mountain tsunami that collapsed the shrine building, so it was moved to Mount Tarumi the following year.

The shrine preserves as many as 104 munafuda dating from 1650 onward. These plaques show that even in the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, when commoners were not permitted {{jalink|wearing surnames and swords|Q11616116|surnames and sword-bearing}}, residents used {{jalink|myōji|Q69634220|surnames}} in shrine activities and lived actively.

=== References ===

* ''Shizu no Kutsu'' by Ishikawa Katsusada, 1917, NCID BA32916118

* ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol. 21'', Shikinaisha Research Society, 1976, NCID BN00231541

* ''Origins of Shizuma Shrine and Its Torii'' by Isao Sawa, Katsunori Ishikawa, and Yoshiaki Koizuka, Cave Environment NET Society, 2013, ISSN 1884-2402

=== Notes ===
{{脚注ヘルプ}}<references />

After damage from high waves, it once moved outside the cave, then a heavy rain in June {{jalink|1674|Q7620}} ({{jalink|Enpō|Q1145735}} 2) caused a debris-flow mountain tsunami that collapsed the shrine building, so it was moved to Mount Tarumi the following year.

== Japanese content ==
{{出典の明記|date=November 2022}}
{{神社
|名称=Shizuma Shrine
|画像={{画像募集中|cat=Ōda}}
|所在地={{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Ōda|Q384796}} Shizuma-chō Tarumi 1765
|位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|祭神={{jalink|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}} and {{jalink|Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}}
|例祭=April 15
|創建={{jalink|886|Q24683}} ({{jalink|Ninna|Q1346317}} 2)
|地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Shizuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Shizuma Shrine''' (しずまじんじゃ) is an ancient shrine in Shizuma-chō, {{jalink|Ōda|Q384796}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}, associated with the “Shizu no Iwaya” sung in the {{jalink|Man'yōshū|Q834321}}, and is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>.

=== Explanation ===
It was founded in {{jalink|Ninna|Q1346317}} 2 (886), and the current main sanctuary is built in Kasuga-zukuri style.

The main deities are {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}} and {{jalink|Sukunabikona|Q3275687|Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}}. Since the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} period, two Hachimangū shrines, two Itsukushima shrines, Ōtoshi Shrine, Kareki Shrine, and others formerly in the village have been merged and enshrined in the main sanctuary.

As precinct shrines, there is Tenmangū on the left (formerly Tarumi Hachimangū, but after merger with Kami-Shizuma Hachimangū and transfer to the main sanctuary, it now enshrines the Tenjin deity that had stood before the waterfall); on the right is Tōshōgū (because it was former {{jalink|tenryō|Q1685411}}, it enshrines {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}} and {{jalink|Mōri Terumoto|Q1142657}}); and in the pond is Suijin Shrine (formerly Ukanomitama Shrine, with {{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Ukanomitama}} as deity, now enshrined in the main sanctuary; an Itsukushima Shrine is also presumed to have been here). The {{jalink|annual festival|Q11385469}} is April 15 (until the Meiji period, it was on the 15th days of the 3rd and 9th lunar months).

At the time of foundation, the shrine was enshrined inside {{jalink|Shizu no Kutsu|Q11662872}}, associated with the Man'yōshū, in the nearby Uozu settlement. After damage from high waves, it once moved outside the cave, then a heavy rain in June {{jalink|1674|Q7620}} ({{jalink|Enpō|Q1145735}} 2) caused a debris-flow mountain tsunami that collapsed the shrine building, so it was moved to Mount Tarumi the following year.

The shrine preserves as many as 104 munafuda dating from 1650 onward. These plaques show that even in the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, when commoners were not permitted {{jalink|wearing surnames and swords|Q11616116|surnames and sword-bearing}}, residents used {{jalink|myōji|Q69634220|surnames}} in shrine activities and lived actively.

=== References ===

* ''Shizu no Kutsu'' by Ishikawa Katsusada, 1917, NCID BA32916118

* ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol. 21'', Shikinaisha Research Society, 1976, NCID BN00231541

* ''Origins of Shizuma Shrine and Its Torii'' by Isao Sawa, Katsunori Ishikawa, and Yoshiaki Koizuka, Cave Environment NET Society, 2013, ISSN 1884-2402

=== Notes ===
{{脚注ヘルプ}}<references />
=== Related items ===
* {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Iwami Province|Q11587179}}

{{Shinto-stub}}

== Japanese content ==
{{出典の明記|date=November 2022}}
{{神社
|名称=Shizuma Shrine
|画像={{画像募集中|cat=Ōda}}
|所在地={{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Ōda|Q384796}} Shizuma-chō Tarumi 1765
|位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|祭神={{jalink|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}} and {{jalink|Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}}
|例祭=April 15
|創建={{jalink|886|Q24683}} ({{jalink|Ninna|Q1346317}} 2)
|地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Shizuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Shizuma Shrine''' (しずまじんじゃ) is an ancient shrine in Shizuma-chō, {{jalink|Ōda|Q384796}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}, associated with the “Shizu no Iwaya” sung in the {{jalink|Man'yōshū|Q834321}}, and is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>.

=== Explanation ===
It was founded in {{jalink|Ninna|Q1346317}} 2 (886), and the current main sanctuary is built in Kasuga-zukuri style.

The main deities are {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}} and {{jalink|Sukunabikona|Q3275687|Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}}. Since the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} period, two Hachimangū shrines, two Itsukushima shrines, Ōtoshi Shrine, Kareki Shrine, and others formerly in the village have been merged and enshrined in the main sanctuary.

As precinct shrines, there is Tenmangū on the left (formerly Tarumi Hachimangū, but after merger with Kami-Shizuma Hachimangū and transfer to the main sanctuary, it now enshrines the Tenjin deity that had stood before the waterfall); on the right is Tōshōgū (because it was former {{jalink|tenryō|Q1685411}}, it enshrines {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}} and {{jalink|Mōri Terumoto|Q1142657}}); and in the pond is Suijin Shrine (formerly Ukanomitama Shrine, with {{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Ukanomitama}} as deity, now enshrined in the main sanctuary; an Itsukushima Shrine is also presumed to have been here). The {{jalink|annual festival|Q11385469}} is April 15 (until the Meiji period, it was on the 15th days of the 3rd and 9th lunar months).

At the time of foundation, the shrine was enshrined inside {{jalink|Shizu no Kutsu|Q11662872}}, associated with the Man'yōshū, in the nearby Uozu settlement. After damage from high waves, it once moved outside the cave, then a heavy rain in June {{jalink|1674|Q7620}} ({{jalink|Enpō|Q1145735}} 2) caused a debris-flow mountain tsunami that collapsed the shrine building, so it was moved to Mount Tarumi the following year.

The shrine preserves as many as 104 munafuda dating from 1650 onward. These plaques show that even in the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, when commoners were not permitted {{jalink|wearing surnames and swords|Q11616116|surnames and sword-bearing}}, residents used {{jalink|myōji|Q69634220|surnames}} in shrine activities and lived actively.

=== References ===

* ''Shizu no Kutsu'' by Ishikawa Katsusada, 1917, NCID BA32916118

* ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol. 21'', Shikinaisha Research Society, 1976, NCID BN00231541

* ''Origins of Shizuma Shrine and Its Torii'' by Isao Sawa, Katsunori Ishikawa, and Yoshiaki Koizuka, Cave Environment NET Society, 2013, ISSN 1884-2402

=== Notes ===
{{脚注ヘルプ}}<references />
=== Related items ===
* {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Iwami Province|Q11587179}}

{{Shinto-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:しすましんしや}}

As precinct shrines, there is Tenmangū on the left (formerly Tarumi Hachimangū, but after merger with Kami-Shizuma Hachimangū and transfer to the main sanctuary, it now enshrines the Tenjin deity that had stood before the waterfall); on the right is Tōshōgū (because it was former {{ill|lt=tenryō|Q1685411|WD=Q1685411}}, it enshrines {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977}} and {{ill|lt=Mōri Terumoto|Q1142657|WD=Q1142657}}); and in the pond is Suijin Shrine (formerly Ukanomitama Shrine, with {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama}} as deity, now enshrined in the main sanctuary; an Itsukushima Shrine is also presumed to have been here). The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469}} is April 15 (until the Meiji period, it was on the 15th days of the 3rd and 9th lunar months).

At the time of foundation, the shrine was enshrined inside {{ill|lt=Shizu no Kutsu|Q11662872|WD=Q11662872}}, associated with the Man'yōshū, in the nearby Uozu settlement. After damage from high waves, it once moved outside the cave, then a heavy rain in June {{ill|lt=1674|Q7620|WD=Q7620}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} 2) caused a debris-flow mountain tsunami that collapsed the shrine building, so it was moved to Mount Tarumi the following year.

The shrine preserves as many as 104 munafuda dating from 1650 onward. These plaques show that even in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, when commoners were not permitted {{ill|lt=wearing surnames and swords|Q11616116|WD=Q11616116|lt=surnames and sword-bearing}}, residents used {{ill|lt=myōji|Q69634220|WD=Q69634220|lt=surnames}} in shrine activities and lived actively.

=== References ===

* ''Shizu no Kutsu'' by Ishikawa Katsusada, 1917, NCID BA32916118

* ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol. 21'', Shikinaisha Research Society, 1976, NCID BN00231541

* ''Origins of Shizuma Shrine and Its Torii'' by Isao Sawa, Katsunori Ishikawa, and Yoshiaki Koizuka, Cave Environment NET Society, 2013, ISSN 1884-2402

'''Shizuma Shrine''' (しずまじんじゃ) is an ancient shrine in Shizuma-chō, {{ill|lt=Ōda|Q384796|WD=Q384796}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}, associated with the “Shizu no Iwaya” sung in the {{ill|lt=Man'yōshū|Q834321|WD=Q834321}}, and is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>.

=== Explanation ===
It was founded in {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317}} 2 (886), and the current main sanctuary is built in Kasuga-zukuri style.

The main deities are {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}} and {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}}. Since the {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} period, two Hachimangū shrines, two Itsukushima shrines, Ōtoshi Shrine, Kareki Shrine, and others formerly in the village have been merged and enshrined in the main sanctuary.

As precinct shrines, there is Tenmangū on the left (formerly Tarumi Hachimangū, but after merger with Kami-Shizuma Hachimangū and transfer to the main sanctuary, it now enshrines the Tenjin deity that had stood before the waterfall); on the right is Tōshōgū (because it was former {{ill|lt=tenryō|Q1685411|WD=Q1685411}}, it enshrines {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977}} and {{ill|lt=Mōri Terumoto|Q1142657|WD=Q1142657}}); and in the pond is Suijin Shrine (formerly Ukanomitama Shrine, with {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama}} as deity, now enshrined in the main sanctuary; an Itsukushima Shrine is also presumed to have been here). The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469}} is April 15 (until the Meiji period, it was on the 15th days of the 3rd and 9th lunar months).

At the time of foundation, the shrine was enshrined inside {{ill|lt=Shizu no Kutsu|Q11662872|WD=Q11662872}}, associated with the Man'yōshū, in the nearby Uozu settlement. After damage from high waves, it once moved outside the cave, then a heavy rain in June {{ill|lt=1674|Q7620|WD=Q7620}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} 2) caused a debris-flow mountain tsunami that collapsed the shrine building, so it was moved to Mount Tarumi the following year.

The shrine preserves as many as 104 munafuda dating from 1650 onward. These plaques show that even in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, when commoners were not permitted {{ill|lt=wearing surnames and swords|Q11616116|WD=Q11616116|lt=surnames and sword-bearing}}, residents used {{ill|lt=myōji|Q69634220|WD=Q69634220|lt=surnames}} in shrine activities and lived actively.

=== References ===

== Japanese content ==
{{出典の明記|date=November 2022}}
{{神社
|名称=Shizuma Shrine
|画像={{画像募集中|cat=Ōda}}
|所在地={{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}{{ill|lt=Ōda|Q384796|WD=Q384796}} Shizuma-chō Tarumi 1765
|位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|祭神={{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
|例祭=April 15
|創建={{ill|lt=886|Q24683|WD=Q24683}} ({{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317}} 2)
|地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Shizuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Shizuma Shrine''' (しずまじんじゃ) is an ancient shrine in Shizuma-chō, {{ill|lt=Ōda|Q384796|WD=Q384796}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}, associated with the “Shizu no Iwaya” sung in the {{ill|lt=Man'yōshū|Q834321|WD=Q834321}}, and is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>.

=== Explanation ===
It was founded in {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317}} 2 (886), and the current main sanctuary is built in Kasuga-zukuri style.

The main deities are {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}} and {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}}. Since the {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} period, two Hachimangū shrines, two Itsukushima shrines, Ōtoshi Shrine, Kareki Shrine, and others formerly in the village have been merged and enshrined in the main sanctuary.

As precinct shrines, there is Tenmangū on the left (formerly Tarumi Hachimangū, but after merger with Kami-Shizuma Hachimangū and transfer to the main sanctuary, it now enshrines the Tenjin deity that had stood before the waterfall); on the right is Tōshōgū (because it was former {{ill|lt=tenryō|Q1685411|WD=Q1685411}}, it enshrines {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977}} and {{ill|lt=Mōri Terumoto|Q1142657|WD=Q1142657}}); and in the pond is Suijin Shrine (formerly Ukanomitama Shrine, with {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama}} as deity, now enshrined in the main sanctuary; an Itsukushima Shrine is also presumed to have been here). The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469}} is April 15 (until the Meiji period, it was on the 15th days of the 3rd and 9th lunar months).

At the time of foundation, the shrine was enshrined inside {{ill|lt=Shizu no Kutsu|Q11662872|WD=Q11662872}}, associated with the Man'yōshū, in the nearby Uozu settlement. After damage from high waves, it once moved outside the cave, then a heavy rain in June {{ill|lt=1674|Q7620|WD=Q7620}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} 2) caused a debris-flow mountain tsunami that collapsed the shrine building, so it was moved to Mount Tarumi the following year.

The shrine preserves as many as 104 munafuda dating from 1650 onward. These plaques show that even in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, when commoners were not permitted {{ill|lt=wearing surnames and swords|Q11616116|WD=Q11616116|lt=surnames and sword-bearing}}, residents used {{ill|lt=myōji|Q69634220|WD=Q69634220|lt=surnames}} in shrine activities and lived actively.

=== References ===

* ''Shizu no Kutsu'' by Ishikawa Katsusada, 1917, NCID BA32916118

* ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol. 21'', Shikinaisha Research Society, 1976, NCID BN00231541

* ''Origins of Shizuma Shrine and Its Torii'' by Isao Sawa, Katsunori Ishikawa, and Yoshiaki Koizuka, Cave Environment NET Society, 2013, ISSN 1884-2402

The main deities are {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}} and {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}}. Since the {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} period, two Hachimangū shrines, two Itsukushima shrines, Ōtoshi Shrine, Kareki Shrine, and others formerly in the village have been merged and enshrined in the main sanctuary.

As precinct shrines, there is Tenmangū on the left (formerly Tarumi Hachimangū, but after merger with Kami-Shizuma Hachimangū and transfer to the main sanctuary, it now enshrines the Tenjin deity that had stood before the waterfall); on the right is Tōshōgū (because it was former {{ill|lt=tenryō|Q1685411|WD=Q1685411}}, it enshrines {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977}} and {{ill|lt=Mōri Terumoto|Q1142657|WD=Q1142657}}); and in the pond is Suijin Shrine (formerly Ukanomitama Shrine, with {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama}} as deity, now enshrined in the main sanctuary; an Itsukushima Shrine is also presumed to have been here). The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469}} is April 15 (until the Meiji period, it was on the 15th days of the 3rd and 9th lunar months).

At the time of foundation, the shrine was enshrined inside {{ill|lt=Shizu no Kutsu|Q11662872|WD=Q11662872}}, associated with the Man'yōshū, in the nearby Uozu settlement. After damage from high waves, it once moved outside the cave, then a heavy rain in June {{ill|lt=1674|Q7620|WD=Q7620}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} 2) caused a debris-flow mountain tsunami that collapsed the shrine building, so it was moved to Mount Tarumi the following year.

The shrine preserves as many as 104 munafuda dating from 1650 onward. These plaques show that even in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, when commoners were not permitted {{ill|lt=wearing surnames and swords|Q11616116|WD=Q11616116|lt=surnames and sword-bearing}}, residents used {{ill|lt=myōji|Q69634220|WD=Q69634220|lt=surnames}} in shrine activities and lived actively.

It was founded in {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317}} 2 (886), and the current main sanctuary is built in Kasuga-zukuri style.

The main deities are {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto}} and {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto}}. The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469}} is April 15 (until the Meiji period, it was on the 15th days of the 3rd and 9th lunar months).

At the time of foundation, the shrine was enshrined inside {{ill|lt=Shizu no Kutsu|Q11662872|WD=Q11662872}}, associated with the Man'yōshū, in the nearby Uozu settlement. These plaques show that even in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, when commoners were not permitted {{ill|lt=wearing surnames and swords|Q11616116|WD=Q11616116|lt=surnames and sword-bearing}}, residents used {{ill|lt=myōji|Q69634220|WD=Q69634220|lt=surnames}} in shrine activities and lived actively.

== Japanese content ==
{{出典の明記|date=November 2022}}
{{神社
|名称=Shizuma Shrine
|画像={{画像募集中|cat=Ōda}}
|所在地={{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Ōda|Q384796|WD=Q384796|1=Ōda, Shimane}} Shizuma-chō Tarumi 1765
|位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|祭神={{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}
|例祭=April 15
|創建={{ill|lt=886|Q24683|WD=Q24683|1=886}} ({{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} 2)
|地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Shizuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Shizuma Shrine''' (しずまじんじゃ) is an ancient shrine in Shizuma-chō, {{ill|lt=Ōda|Q384796|WD=Q384796|1=Ōda, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, associated with the “Shizu no Iwaya” sung in the {{ill|lt=Man'yōshū|Q834321|WD=Q834321|1=Man'yōshū}}, and is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>.

=== Explanation ===
It was founded in {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} 2 (886), and the current main sanctuary is built in Kasuga-zukuri style.

The main deities are {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}} and {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}}. Since the {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period, two Hachimangū shrines, two Itsukushima shrines, Ōtoshi Shrine, Kareki Shrine, and others formerly in the village have been merged and enshrined in the main sanctuary.

As precinct shrines, there is Tenmangū on the left (formerly Tarumi Hachimangū, but after merger with Kami-Shizuma Hachimangū and transfer to the main sanctuary, it now enshrines the Tenjin deity that had stood before the waterfall); on the right is Tōshōgū (because it was former {{ill|lt=tenryō|Q1685411|WD=Q1685411|1=Tenryō}}, it enshrines {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} and {{ill|lt=Mōri Terumoto|Q1142657|WD=Q1142657|1=Mōri Terumoto}}); and in the pond is Suijin Shrine (formerly Ukanomitama Shrine, with {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|1=Ukanomitama}} as deity, now enshrined in the main sanctuary; an Itsukushima Shrine is also presumed to have been here). The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is April 15 (until the Meiji period, it was on the 15th days of the 3rd and 9th lunar months).

At the time of foundation, the shrine was enshrined inside {{ill|lt=Shizu no Kutsu|Q11662872|WD=Q11662872|1=Shizu no Iwaya}}, associated with the Man'yōshū, in the nearby Uozu settlement. After damage from high waves, it once moved outside the cave, then a heavy rain in June {{ill|lt=1674|Q7620|WD=Q7620|1=1674}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 2) caused a debris-flow mountain tsunami that collapsed the shrine building, so it was moved to Mount Tarumi the following year.

The shrine preserves as many as 104 munafuda dating from 1650 onward. These plaques show that even in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, when commoners were not permitted {{ill|lt=wearing surnames and swords|Q11616116|WD=Q11616116|lt=surnames and sword-bearing|1=myoujitaitou}}, residents used {{ill|lt=myōji|Q69634220|WD=Q69634220|lt=surnames|1=myōji}} in shrine activities and lived actively.

=== References ===

* ''Shizu no Kutsu'' by Ishikawa Katsusada, 1917, NCID BA32916118

* ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol. 21'', Shikinaisha Research Society, 1976, NCID BN00231541

* ''Origins of Shizuma Shrine and Its Torii'' by Isao Sawa, Katsunori Ishikawa, and Yoshiaki Koizuka, Cave Environment NET Society, 2013, ISSN 1884-2402

=== Notes ===

|例祭=April 15

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Shizuma Shrine''' (しずまじんじゃ) is an ancient shrine in Shizuma-chō, {{ill|lt=Ōda|Q384796|WD=Q384796|1=Ōda, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, associated with the “Shizu no Iwaya” sung in the {{ill|lt=Man'yōshū|Q834321|WD=Q834321|1=Man'yōshū}}, and is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>.

=== Explanation ===
It was founded in {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} 2 (886), and the current main sanctuary is built in Kasuga-zukuri style.

The main deities are {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}} and {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}}. Since the {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period, two Hachimangū shrines, two Itsukushima shrines, Ōtoshi Shrine, Kareki Shrine, and others formerly in the village have been merged and enshrined in the main sanctuary.

As precinct shrines, there is Tenmangū on the left (formerly Tarumi Hachimangū, but after merger with Kami-Shizuma Hachimangū and transfer to the main sanctuary, it now enshrines the Tenjin deity that had stood before the waterfall); on the right is Tōshōgū (because it was former {{ill|lt=tenryō|Q1685411|WD=Q1685411|1=Tenryō}}, it enshrines {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} and {{ill|lt=Mōri Terumoto|Q1142657|WD=Q1142657|1=Mōri Terumoto}}); and in the pond is Suijin Shrine (formerly Ukanomitama Shrine, with {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|1=Ukanomitama}} as deity, now enshrined in the main sanctuary; an Itsukushima Shrine is also presumed to have been here). The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is April 15 (until the Meiji period, it was on the 15th days of the 3rd and 9th lunar months).

At the time of foundation, the shrine was enshrined inside {{ill|lt=Shizu no Kutsu|Q11662872|WD=Q11662872|1=Shizu no Iwaya}}, associated with the Man'yōshū, in the nearby Uozu settlement. After damage from high waves, it once moved outside the cave, then a heavy rain in June {{ill|lt=1674|Q7620|WD=Q7620|1=1674}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 2) caused a debris-flow mountain tsunami that collapsed the shrine building, so it was moved to Mount Tarumi the following year.

The shrine preserves as many as 104 munafuda dating from 1650 onward. These plaques show that even in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, when commoners were not permitted {{ill|lt=wearing surnames and swords|Q11616116|WD=Q11616116|lt=surnames and sword-bearing|1=myoujitaitou}}, residents used {{ill|lt=myōji|Q69634220|WD=Q69634220|lt=surnames|1=myōji}} in shrine activities and lived actively.

=== References ===

* ''Shizu no Kutsu'' by Ishikawa Katsusada, 1917, NCID BA32916118

* ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol. 21'', Shikinaisha Research Society, 1976, NCID BN00231541

* ''Origins of Shizuma Shrine and Its Torii'' by Isao Sawa, Katsunori Ishikawa, and Yoshiaki Koizuka, Cave Environment NET Society, 2013, ISSN 1884-2402

The main deities are {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}} and {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}}. Since the {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period, two Hachimangū shrines, two Itsukushima shrines, Ōtoshi Shrine, Kareki Shrine, and others formerly in the village have been merged and enshrined in the main sanctuary.

As precinct shrines, there is Tenmangū on the left (formerly Tarumi Hachimangū, but after merger with Kami-Shizuma Hachimangū and transfer to the main sanctuary, it now enshrines the Tenjin deity that had stood before the waterfall); on the right is Tōshōgū (because it was former {{ill|lt=tenryō|Q1685411|WD=Q1685411|1=Tenryō}}, it enshrines {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} and {{ill|lt=Mōri Terumoto|Q1142657|WD=Q1142657|1=Mōri Terumoto}}); and in the pond is Suijin Shrine (formerly Ukanomitama Shrine, with {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|1=Ukanomitama}} as deity, now enshrined in the main sanctuary; an Itsukushima Shrine is also presumed to have been here). The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is April 15 (until the Meiji period, it was on the 15th days of the 3rd and 9th lunar months).

At the time of foundation, the shrine was enshrined inside {{ill|lt=Shizu no Kutsu|Q11662872|WD=Q11662872|1=Shizu no Iwaya}}, associated with the Man'yōshū, in the nearby Uozu settlement. After damage from high waves, it once moved outside the cave, then a heavy rain in June {{ill|lt=1674|Q7620|WD=Q7620|1=1674}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 2) caused a debris-flow mountain tsunami that collapsed the shrine building, so it was moved to Mount Tarumi the following year.

The shrine preserves as many as 104 munafuda dating from 1650 onward. These plaques show that even in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, when commoners were not permitted {{ill|lt=wearing surnames and swords|Q11616116|WD=Q11616116|lt=surnames and sword-bearing|1=myoujitaitou}}, residents used {{ill|lt=myōji|Q69634220|WD=Q69634220|lt=surnames|1=myōji}} in shrine activities and lived actively.

=== References ===

* ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol. 21'', Shikinaisha Research Society, 1976, NCID BN00231541

* ''Origins of Shizuma Shrine and Its Torii'' by Isao Sawa, Katsunori Ishikawa, and Yoshiaki Koizuka, Cave Environment NET Society, 2013, ISSN 1884-2402

'''Shizuma Shrine''' (しずまじんじゃ) is an ancient shrine in Shizuma-chō, {{ill|lt=Ōda|Q384796|WD=Q384796|1=Ōda, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, associated with the “Shizu no Iwaya” sung in the {{ill|lt=Man'yōshū|Q834321|WD=Q834321|1=Man'yōshū}}, and is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>.

=== Explanation ===
It was founded in {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} 2 (886), and the current main sanctuary is built in Kasuga-zukuri style.

The main deities are {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}} and {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}}. Since the {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period, two Hachimangū shrines, two Itsukushima shrines, Ōtoshi Shrine, Kareki Shrine, and others formerly in the village have been merged and enshrined in the main sanctuary.

As precinct shrines, there is Tenmangū on the left (formerly Tarumi Hachimangū, but after merger with Kami-Shizuma Hachimangū and transfer to the main sanctuary, it now enshrines the Tenjin deity that had stood before the waterfall); on the right is Tōshōgū (because it was former {{ill|lt=tenryō|Q1685411|WD=Q1685411|1=Tenryō}}, it enshrines {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} and {{ill|lt=Mōri Terumoto|Q1142657|WD=Q1142657|1=Mōri Terumoto}}); and in the pond is Suijin Shrine (formerly Ukanomitama Shrine, with {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|1=Ukanomitama}} as deity, now enshrined in the main sanctuary; an Itsukushima Shrine is also presumed to have been here). The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is April 15 (until the Meiji period, it was on the 15th days of the 3rd and 9th lunar months).

At the time of foundation, the shrine was enshrined inside {{ill|lt=Shizu no Kutsu|Q11662872|WD=Q11662872|1=Shizu no Iwaya}}, associated with the Man'yōshū, in the nearby Uozu settlement. After damage from high waves, it once moved outside the cave, then a heavy rain in June {{ill|lt=1674|Q7620|WD=Q7620|1=1674}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 2) caused a debris-flow mountain tsunami that collapsed the shrine building, so it was moved to Mount Tarumi the following year.

The shrine preserves as many as 104 munafuda dating from 1650 onward. These plaques show that even in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, when commoners were not permitted {{ill|lt=wearing surnames and swords|Q11616116|WD=Q11616116|lt=surnames and sword-bearing|1=myoujitaitou}}, residents used {{ill|lt=myōji|Q69634220|WD=Q69634220|lt=surnames|1=myōji}} in shrine activities and lived actively.

{{nihongo|'''Shizuma Shrine'''|静間神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Iwami Province]].

== Japanese content ==
{{出典の明記|date=November 2022}}
{{神社
|名称=Shizuma Shrine
|画像={{画像募集中|cat=Ōda}}
|所在地={{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Ōda|Q384796|WD=Q384796|1=Ōda, Shimane}} Shizuma-chō Tarumi 1765
|位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}}
|祭神={{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}
|例祭=April 15
|創建={{ill|lt=886|Q24683|WD=Q24683|1=886}} ({{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} 2)
|地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Shizuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}
}}
'''Shizuma Shrine''' (しずまじんじゃ) is an ancient shrine in Shizuma-chō, {{ill|lt=Ōda|Q384796|WD=Q384796|1=Ōda, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, associated with the “Shizu no Iwaya” sung in the {{ill|lt=Man'yōshū|Q834321|WD=Q834321|1=Man'yōshū}}, and is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>.

=== Explanation ===
It was founded in {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} 2 (886), and the current main sanctuary is built in Kasuga-zukuri style.

The main deities are {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}} and {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}}. Since the {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period, two Hachimangū shrines, two Itsukushima shrines, Ōtoshi Shrine, Kareki Shrine, and others formerly in the village have been merged and enshrined in the main sanctuary.

As precinct shrines, there is Tenmangū on the left (formerly Tarumi Hachimangū, but after merger with Kami-Shizuma Hachimangū and transfer to the main sanctuary, it now enshrines the Tenjin deity that had stood before the waterfall); on the right is Tōshōgū (because it was former {{ill|lt=tenryō|Q1685411|WD=Q1685411|1=Tenryō}}, it enshrines {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} and {{ill|lt=Mōri Terumoto|Q1142657|WD=Q1142657|1=Mōri Terumoto}}); and in the pond is Suijin Shrine (formerly Ukanomitama Shrine, with {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|1=Ukanomitama}} as deity, now enshrined in the main sanctuary; an Itsukushima Shrine is also presumed to have been here). The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is April 15 (until the Meiji period, it was on the 15th days of the 3rd and 9th lunar months).

At the time of foundation, the shrine was enshrined inside {{ill|lt=Shizu no Kutsu|Q11662872|WD=Q11662872|1=Shizu no Iwaya}}, associated with the Man'yōshū, in the nearby Uozu settlement. After damage from high waves, it once moved outside the cave, then a heavy rain in June {{ill|lt=1674|Q7620|WD=Q7620|1=1674}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 2) caused a debris-flow mountain tsunami that collapsed the shrine building, so it was moved to Mount Tarumi the following year.

The shrine preserves as many as 104 munafuda dating from 1650 onward. These plaques show that even in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, when commoners were not permitted {{ill|lt=wearing surnames and swords|Q11616116|WD=Q11616116|lt=surnames and sword-bearing|1=myoujitaitou}}, residents used {{ill|lt=myōji|Q69634220|WD=Q69634220|lt=surnames|1=myōji}} in shrine activities and lived actively.

=== References ===

* ''Shizu no Kutsu'' by Ishikawa Katsusada, 1917, NCID BA32916118

* ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol. 21'', Shikinaisha Research Society, 1976, NCID BN00231541

* ''Origins of Shizuma Shrine and Its Torii'' by Isao Sawa, Katsunori Ishikawa, and Yoshiaki Koizuka, Cave Environment NET Society, 2013, ISSN 1884-2402

=== Notes ===
{{脚注ヘルプ}}<references />
=== Related items ===

{{Shinto-stub}}

'''Shizuma Shrine''' (しずまじんじゃ) is an ancient shrine in Shizuma-chō, {{ill|lt=Ōda|Q384796|WD=Q384796|1=Ōda, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, associated with the “Shizu no Iwaya” sung in the {{ill|lt=Man'yōshū|Q834321|WD=Q834321|1=Man'yōshū}}, and is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>.

=== Explanation ===
It was founded in {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} 2 (886), and the current main sanctuary is built in Kasuga-zukuri style.

The main deities are {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}} and {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}}. Since the {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period, two Hachimangū shrines, two Itsukushima shrines, Ōtoshi Shrine, Kareki Shrine, and others formerly in the village have been merged and enshrined in the main sanctuary.

As precinct shrines, there is Tenmangū on the left (formerly Tarumi Hachimangū, but after merger with Kami-Shizuma Hachimangū and transfer to the main sanctuary, it now enshrines the Tenjin deity that had stood before the waterfall); on the right is Tōshōgū (because it was former {{ill|lt=tenryō|Q1685411|WD=Q1685411|1=Tenryō}}, it enshrines {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} and {{ill|lt=Mōri Terumoto|Q1142657|WD=Q1142657|1=Mōri Terumoto}}); and in the pond is Suijin Shrine (formerly Ukanomitama Shrine, with {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|1=Ukanomitama}} as deity, now enshrined in the main sanctuary; an Itsukushima Shrine is also presumed to have been here). The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is April 15 (until the Meiji period, it was on the 15th days of the 3rd and 9th lunar months).

At the time of foundation, the shrine was enshrined inside {{ill|lt=Shizu no Kutsu|Q11662872|WD=Q11662872|1=Shizu no Iwaya}}, associated with the Man'yōshū, in the nearby Uozu settlement. After damage from high waves, it once moved outside the cave, then a heavy rain in June {{ill|lt=1674|Q7620|WD=Q7620|1=1674}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 2) caused a debris-flow mountain tsunami that collapsed the shrine building, so it was moved to Mount Tarumi the following year.

The shrine preserves as many as 104 munafuda dating from 1650 onward. These plaques show that even in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, when commoners were not permitted {{ill|lt=wearing surnames and swords|Q11616116|WD=Q11616116|lt=surnames and sword-bearing|1=myoujitaitou}}, residents used {{ill|lt=myōji|Q69634220|WD=Q69634220|lt=surnames|1=myōji}} in shrine activities and lived actively.

* ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol. 21'', Shikinaisha Research Society, 1976, NCID BN00231541

'''Shizuma Shrine''' (しずまじんじゃ) is an ancient shrine in Shizuma-chō, {{ill|lt=Ōda|Q384796|WD=Q384796|1=Ōda, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, associated with the “Shizu no Iwaya” sung in the {{ill|lt=Man'yōshū|Q834321|WD=Q834321|1=Man'yōshū}}, and is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}<ref>http://www.cave-ens.com/pdf/CENS4_004.pdf</ref>.

=== Explanation ===
It was founded in {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|WD=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} 2 (886), and the current main sanctuary is built in Kasuga-zukuri style.

The main deities are {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}} and {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}}. Since the {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period, two Hachimangū shrines, two Itsukushima shrines, Ōtoshi Shrine, Kareki Shrine, and others formerly in the village have been merged and enshrined in the main sanctuary.

As precinct shrines, there is Tenmangū on the left (formerly Tarumi Hachimangū, but after merger with Kami-Shizuma Hachimangū and transfer to the main sanctuary, it now enshrines the Tenjin deity that had stood before the waterfall); on the right is Tōshōgū (because it was former {{ill|lt=tenryō|Q1685411|WD=Q1685411|1=Tenryō}}, it enshrines {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} and {{ill|lt=Mōri Terumoto|Q1142657|WD=Q1142657|1=Mōri Terumoto}}); and in the pond is Suijin Shrine (formerly Ukanomitama Shrine, with {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|1=Ukanomitama}} as deity, now enshrined in the main sanctuary; an Itsukushima Shrine is also presumed to have been here). The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is April 15 (until the Meiji period, it was on the 15th days of the 3rd and 9th lunar months).

At the time of foundation, the shrine was enshrined inside {{ill|lt=Shizu no Kutsu|Q11662872|WD=Q11662872|1=Shizu no Iwaya}}, associated with the Man'yōshū, in the nearby Uozu settlement. After damage from high waves, it once moved outside the cave, then a heavy rain in June {{ill|lt=1674|Q7620|WD=Q7620|1=1674}} ({{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 2) caused a debris-flow mountain tsunami that collapsed the shrine building, so it was moved to Mount Tarumi the following year.

The shrine preserves as many as 104 munafuda dating from 1650 onward. These plaques show that even in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, when commoners were not permitted {{ill|lt=wearing surnames and swords|Q11616116|WD=Q11616116|lt=surnames and sword-bearing|1=myoujitaitou}}, residents used {{ill|lt=myōji|Q69634220|WD=Q69634220|lt=surnames|1=myōji}} in shrine activities and lived actively.

=== References ===
